fact: June 19 is exactly one day before the summer solstice in the Northern Hemisphere, making it the second-longest day of the year!
In Alaska, the sun stays up for nearly 22 hours on this date - that's like having daylight from breakfast until way past your bedtime!
Fireflies typically start their blinking dance parties around this time in North America, turning backyards into natural light shows.
June 19 usually falls in the middle of peak strawberry season in northern climates, when the berries taste extra sweet!
Baby birds are often learning to fly around this date in North America, which means lots of clumsy crash landings and confused chirping in neighborhoods.
June 19 falls under the zodiac sign Gemini, since the sign switches to Cancer on June 21.
Mosquitoes are typically at their most active peak around this date in temperate regions because the warm, humid weather makes them super energetic.
In the Southern Hemisphere, June 19 is deep winter, with some places getting only 8 hours of daylight - the complete opposite of Alaska!
Rose bushes often produce their biggest, most colorful blooms around this time in northern gardens, making them smell like fancy perfume shops.
The moon can look extra orange or red when it rises on summer evenings like this because you're looking through more of Earth's thick atmosphere.
June 19 is day 170 of the year, which means there are exactly 195 days left - almost like the year is perfectly split in half!
